科罗拉多大学博尔德分校的研究人员已经开发出一种快速、便携、唾液为基础的新冠肺炎检测方法，能够在45分钟内返回结果。这样的测试最终可能会部署在学校和工厂等社区环境中，目前正在努力进行进一步的验证测试，并寻求监管部门的批准。
科罗拉多大学博尔德分校生物前沿研究所(BioFrontiers Institute At CU Boulder)索耶实验室(Sawyer Lab)博士后助理尼古拉斯·迈耶森(Nicholas Meyerson)说，“随着越来越多的人想要接受检测，诊断实验室不堪重负，我们现在面临着严重的检测短缺。”“我们已经开发出一种测试，可以更快地将结果送到人们手中。”
这项测试在周五发布在在线档案馆MedRxiv.org上的预印手稿中进行了描述，旨在进行广泛的筛查，以帮助识别无症状的个体。研究表明，感染病毒但没有明显症状的人占病例的70%，仍然可以传播疾病。在这项新的测试中，使用者在试管中吐痰，加入一种溶液来稳定试管，然后合上盖子，将其交给测试人员。他们通过一个简单的系统来处理它，只需要移液管、加热源和酶混合物即可。
如果样本从粉红色变成黄色，则检测呈阳性。如果没有，那就是负面的。
研究人员说，因为不需要棉签，也不需要花哨的设备，测试不太容易受到积压和供应链短缺的影响。
“到目前为止，每一项已获批准的测试都要求样本，即使是唾液，也要在临床诊断实验室或医生办公室使用先进的设备进行处理。分子细胞和发育生物学系的病毒学家Sara Sawyer教授领导了这项测试的开发，她说：“现在，这可能需要长达9天的时间。”
这项测试基于一项已有20年历史的技术，名为逆转录环介导的等温扩增(RT-LAMP)，例如，以前曾用于在南美偏远地区对蚊子进行寨卡病毒筛查。
一旦样本被采集，它就被加热以释放存在于测试液中的任何病毒基因组。然后，这个样本被加入三个试管，每个试管都包含一种定制的酶混合物，当加热到一定的温度时，当检测到来自SARS-CoV-2的遗传物质时，会发生化学反应。那就是导致新冠肺炎的病毒。
在论文中描述的一个实验中，研究人员进行了所谓的“人为的临床验证”。一名研究人员在实验室的60个唾液样本中添加了30个灭活的SARS-CoV-2。然后，他们将样本洗牌，交给另一位科学家用RT-LAMP技术进行测试。
Meyerson说：“测试以100%的准确率预测了所有阴性样本，30个阳性样本中有29个被准确预测。”他指出，第30次测试的得分为不确定。额外的第二方验证测试目前正在进行中。
作者指出，与在临床实验室进行的测试相比，该测试的敏感度略低。但同样由生物前沿研究所(BioFrontiers Institute)的研究人员进行的另一项计算机建模研究发现，快速周转检测对于遏制大流行甚至比检测敏感性更关键。
该论文的合著者、BioFrontiers主任罗伊·帕克(Roy Parker)说：“我们的模型表明，测试是敏感的还是超敏感的并不那么重要。”这篇论文尚未经过同行审查。“重要的是频繁的检测，检测结果尽快返回，这样可以更快地识别更多的感染者，并可以限制新的感染。”
“那些检测呈阳性的人可以在等待确认检测时进行自我隔离，”索耶说。该研究小组与CU Boulder的Venture Partners合作，创建了一家名为达尔文生物科学(Darwin Biosciences)的分支公司，将该测试商业化。这项测试还没有得到食品和药物管理局的批准，但研究小组已经通过该机构的紧急使用授权计划提交了文书工作。该团队还在与州政府合作，在当地进行进一步的验证测试，并在等待结果之前，加快监管审批，以便在科罗拉多州使用。
州长贾里德·波利斯(Jared Polis)在周四的新闻发布会上说，我们非常兴奋，并为科罗拉多大学的科学家们鼓掌。
该公司还在研发一种名为SickStick的快速、自行检测传染病的方法，它基于不同的技术，包装很像家用验孕。它希望有一天能通过零售店买到它。
索耶说：“虽然我们都对冠状病毒疫苗持非常乐观的态度，但科学家们30年来一直在研究艾滋病毒疫苗，但没有取得成功。”“与此同时，艾滋病大流行向我们表明，普遍的检测可以起到很大的作用。”
note_en:
CU Boulder researchers have developed a rapid, portable, saliva-based COVID-19 test able to return results in 45 minutes. Such a test might eventually be deployable in community settings like schools and factories, and efforts are underway to conduct further validation tests and seek regulatory approval.
 “We are facing a serious testing shortage in this country right now as more people want to get tested and diagnostics labs are overwhelmed,” said Nicholas Meyerson, a postdoctoral associate in the Sawyer Lab at the  BioFrontiers Institute at CU Boulder. “We’ve developed a test that could get results to people much faster.”
   The test, described in a  preprint manuscript posted Friday on the online archive MedRxiv.org, is designed for widespread screening to help identify asymptomatic individuals. Research shows people infected with the virus but with no obvious symptoms make up as many as 70% of cases and can still spread disease. In this new test, a user spits in a tube, adds a solution to stabilize it then closes the lid and hands it off to testing staff. They process it through a simple system requiring little more than pipettes, a heating source and an enzyme mixture.
 If the sample turns from pink to yellow, the test is positive. If it doesn’t, it’s negative.
 Because no swabs are required, and no fancy equipment is needed, the tests are less vulnerable to backlogs and supply chain shortages, the researchers say.
 “Every test that has been approved to date requires that the sample, even if it’s saliva, be processed in a clinical diagnostic lab or at a doctor’s office, using sophisticated equipment. That can take up to nine days right now,” said Professor Sara Sawyer, a virologist in the Department of Molecular Cellular and Developmental Biology who led the development of the test.
  The test is based on a 20-year-old technology known as reverse transcription loop-mediated isothermal amplification (RT-LAMP) previously used, for instance, to screen mosquitoes for the Zika virus in remote regions of South America.
 Once a sample is collected, it is heated to liberate any viral genome present in the test liquid. This sample is then added to three tubes, each containing a custom enzyme mixture which, when heated to a certain temperature, undergoes a chemical reaction when the genetic material from SARS-CoV-2 is detected. That’s the virus that causes COVID-19.
 In one experiment described in the paper, the researchers conducted what is known as a “contrived clinical validation.” One researcher spiked 30 out of 60 saliva samples with inactivated SARS-CoV-2 in the lab. Then they shuffled the samples and gave them to another scientist to test with the RT-LAMP technology.
 “The test predicted with 100% accuracy all of the negative samples, and 29 of 30 positive samples were predicted accurately,” said Meyerson, noting that the 30th test was scored as inconclusive. Additional second-party validation tests are currently underway.
 The authors note that the test is slightly less sensitive than those performed in clinical labs. But a separate computer modeling study, also by researchers at the BioFrontiers Institute, found that quick turnaround for testing is even more critical to curbing the pandemic than test sensitivity is.
 “Our modeling showed that whether a test is sensitive or super-sensitive is not that important,” said BioFrontiers Director Roy Parker, co-author of that  paper, which has not yet been peer reviewed. “What is important is frequent testing, with the test results returned as fast as possible, which identifies more infected people faster and can limit new infections.”
   “Those who test positive could quarantine themselves as they await confirmatory testing,” said Sawyer. The research team, in cooperation with Venture Partners at CU Boulder, has created a spinoff company, Darwin Biosciences, to commercialize the test. The test has not been approved by the Food and Drug Administration yet, but the team has submitted paperwork via the agency's Emergency Use Authorization program. The team is also working with the state to carry out further validation tests locally and, pending those results, expedite regulatory clearance for use in Colorado.
 "We are very excited and applaud the scientists at the University of Colorado," said Governor Jared Polis, during a press briefing on Thursday.
 The company is also working on a rapid, do-it-yourself test for infectious diseases, known as  SickStick, which is based on a different technology and packaged much like an at-home pregnancy test. It hopes to make it available via retail outlets someday.
 “While we are all very optimistic about a coronavirus vaccine, scientists have been working on an HIV vaccine for 30 years without success,” said Sawyer. “Meantime, the HIV pandemic showed us that pervasive testing can make a big difference.”
title: 唾液Covid检测：45分钟内结果
title_en: Saliva based Covid test: results in 45 minutes
